case_name: Susan Stewart, Personal Representative of the Estate of Joanne Hatton v. Arthur Alunday, M.D.

summary.brief: Susan Stewart, Personal Representative of the Estate of Joanne Hatton v. Arthur Alunday, M.D.. Medical malpractice case involving surgery orthopaedic.

evidence_index.text: In March 2008, Joanne Hatton—who was eighty-five-years old with osteoporosis and scoliosis—was living in a nursing home in Greensburg; Dr. Alunday was her doctor.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On March 10, Hatton was admitted to Decatur County Memorial Hospital (“the hospital”) in Greensburg with flu-like symptoms.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Hatton’s preliminary blood-culture results came back on Sunday, March 17; they showed that Hatton had a Staphylococcus (staph) infection.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On March 16, Hatton went to the emergency room complaining of left hip and back pain.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Alunday resumed Hatton’s care on Monday. That morning, additional blood-culture results showed that Hatton’s staph infection was methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA).
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Hatton underwent an MRI on March 27, and it showed scoliosis and extensive degenerative changes but no spinal-compression fracture.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The bone scan, which was performed on April 8, showed evidence of an acute L2 compression fracture.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Although Hatton was initially stable following the procedure, by mid-May she developed a widespread MRSA infection. Hatton died on May 26.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: We affirm the jury’s verdict in his favor.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: The trial court found that Dr. Alunday made a judicial admission but, concluded that the judicial admission was not conclusive and binding.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient presented with flu-like symptoms and was admitted to the hospital. She received intravenous fluids to prevent dehydration and was discharged after four days.

focused_exam.extremities: Red and tender area where IV was inserted

initial_state.initial_working_problem: Suspected skin infection at IV site
critical_findings: Red and tender area at IV site

reveal.result_summary: Staphylococcus infection, later identified as MRSA
reveal.clinical_significance: Indicates bacterial infection requiring targeted antibiotic therapy

requestables.name: MRI
requestables.description: MRI of the spine to investigate back pain

reveal.result_summary: Showed scoliosis and extensive degenerative changes, no spinal-compression fracture or infection
reveal.clinical_significance: Helped rule out spinal compression fracture and infection

requestables.name: Bone Scan
requestables.description: Bone scan to assess for compression fracture

reveal.result_summary: Evidence of an acute L2 compression fracture

timeline_phases.name: Subsequent Hospitalization and Workup
timeline_phases.description: Patient readmitted with suspected infection and back pain

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient with MRSA infection and back pain
decision_points.prompt: Should the possibility of MRSA seeding to the compression fracture be considered?

options.description: Consider MRSA seeding to the compression fracture
options.clinical_reasoning: Standard of care requires considering MRSA spread in such cases

options.description: Do not consider MRSA seeding to the compression fracture
options.clinical_reasoning: Defendant did not initially consider this possibility

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant did not consider MRSA seeding to the compression fracture
actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Focused on osteoporosis as the cause of fracture

expected_action_court.description: Consideration of MRSA seeding should have been part of the differential
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony indicated this was necessary

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to consider MRSA seeding could lead to inadequate treatment
explanation.what_should_have_happened: MRSA seeding should have been considered and ruled out
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

patient_outcome.description: Patient developed widespread MRSA infection and died

key_findings: Jury found in favor of Dr. Alunday
No breach of standard of care established

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Consideration might have led to different management

factual_timeline.event: Patient admitted to hospital with flu-like symptoms
Blood culture ordered due to suspected infection
Bone scan performed showing compression fracture

tests_performed.result: Staphylococcus infection, later identified as MRSA

treatments.outcome: Initially stable, later developed MRSA infection

allegations.allegation: Failure to consider MRSA seeding to compression fracture

court_findings.finding: No breach of standard of care
court_findings.legal_significance: Jury verdict in favor of Dr. Alunday
